old_claim.note: Animal Husbandry provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No animal-husbandry edge remains because soap can use plant oils as well as animal fats, and the source does not make livestock management a prerequisite of the earliest written production evidence.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract: anchors soap production to third-millennium BCE Mesopotamian clay tablets and describes simple ash-and-fat production without asserting a husbandry prerequisite.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The written soap-production evidence does not identify managed livestock as a necessary upstream technology.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A possible animal-fat ingredient cannot make husbandry universal when soap recipes may use plant oils and the cited tablets do not establish herd management.

ontology_before: A possible fat source made livestock husbandry an enabler of all basic soap production.
ontology_after: The written Mesopotamian production record is not tied to one optional lipid supply system.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: soap_making_basic no longer directly depends on animal_husbandry.
would_reject_if: The earliest Mesopotamian soap texts were shown to require only fat from managed domestic animals.
short_reason: Optional animal fat does not establish a husbandry dependency.
